hero.title: Build Your Own Custom Chips
hero.subtitle: Define your own integrated circuits with the Wokwi-compatible Custom Chips API. Write the chip in C, Rust, or AssemblyScript; Velxio compiles it to WebAssembly and runs it on the canvas like any other component — driven by Arduino, ESP32, or RP2040 firmware.
hero.trust: Wokwi-compatible API · Free & open-source · WASM-powered, runs in your browser

build.heading: What can you build?
build.lead: Custom chips fill the gaps between built-in components. If a real IC isn't on the shelf, build a behavioural model and your firmware never knows the difference.
build.sensorBody: Mock a temperature, pressure, or IMU sensor that responds over I²C — useful when you don't have the part yet but want to develop the firmware.
build.bridgeBody: Build a UART-to-I²C bridge, an SPI shift register, a digital-to-analog wrapper, or a custom level translator.
build.logicBody: Glue logic, state machines, address decoders, debouncers — anything you'd otherwise wire up with discrete gates.
build.icBody: Re-create the externally-visible behaviour of a real IC (a 7-segment driver, a stepper controller) without simulating its analog internals.
build.fixturesBody: Inject signals on schedule, capture pin transitions, validate firmware against expected pin-level behaviour.
build.reuseBody: Save the chip to your account, import it into other projects, share with the community.

api.heading: What the API gives you
api.lead: The Velxio Custom Chips runtime is fully Wokwi-compatible, with extras for the SPICE and multi-board environment.
api.pinBody: Read pin state, write digital high/low, configure pull-ups, react to edges with pin-watch callbacks.
api.attrBody: Read user-configurable chip attributes from the property panel — values, strings, numbers — and re-read on change.
api.timersBody: Schedule callbacks at microsecond resolution. The runtime fires them aligned to the simulator clock.
api.i2cBody: Connect to any wire that's part of an I²C bus, respond to start/stop conditions, acknowledge addresses, send / receive bytes.
api.spiBody: Bidirectional SPI with chip-select handling. Implement a shift register, an SPI memory, or a sensor with one byte-stream callback.
api.wasiBody: A small WASI shim lets your C/Rust chip use standard-library calls (printf, string ops, math) without a heavy runtime.

faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: What is a custom chip in Velxio?
faq.a1: A custom chip is a user-defined integrated circuit. You write the chip's logic in C, Rust, or AssemblyScript — Velxio compiles it to WebAssembly, instantiates it on the canvas like any other component, and drives its pins from the simulator.
faq.a2: Velxio implements the Wokwi Custom Chips API — the same API used by Wokwi — so chips you write for Velxio are portable to Wokwi and vice versa. Velxio adds a WASI shim so you can use standard library functions in your chip code.
faq.a3: Behavioural models of real ICs (sensors, decoders, level translators, protocol bridges), custom digital logic, sensor stand-ins for hardware you don't have yet, and protocol-level mocks for testing firmware. Chips can drive pins, read attributes, register timers, and bridge to I²C / SPI buses.
faq.q4: How do I share or reuse a chip?
faq.a4: Save the chip to your Velxio account and reuse it across projects. The chip definition includes its pin layout, attributes, and the compiled WASM — drop it onto any project canvas like a built-in component.
faq.q5: Do I need a backend to run my chip?
faq.a5: No. Compiled chip WASM runs in the same browser tab as the rest of the simulation. Pin updates, attribute reads, and timer ticks are all local.
faq.q6: Can my custom chip talk to an Arduino?
faq.a6: Yes — that is the typical use case. Wire its pins to Arduino GPIOs, SPI, or I²C lines and the firmware on the Arduino interacts with your chip exactly like a real IC.

bottom.title: Build your first custom chip
bottom.body: Open the editor and create a chip in seconds — pick C, Rust, or AssemblyScript.

hero.title: Free Online Electronics Simulator
hero.accent: Build, wire and test in your browser
hero.subtitle: A complete electronics workbench: SPICE-accurate analog parts, 19 simulated microcontrollers (Arduino, ESP32, RP2040, ATtiny85, Raspberry Pi 3), 100+ components, and live instruments — all in one canvas. Free, no install, no account.
hero.trust: Free & open-source · No signup required · Real SPICE + real CPU emulation

inside.heading: What's inside
inside.lead: One tool, every layer of an electronics project — from the resistor on your breadboard to the firmware on your microcontroller.
inside.spiceBody: Real ngspice via WebAssembly. 100+ device cards: passives, BJTs, MOSFETs, op-amps, regulators, diodes, optocouplers, relays.
inside.mcuBody: Arduino Uno / Nano / Mega / ATtiny85 / Leonardo / Pro Mini, Raspberry Pi Pico (W), ESP32 / ESP32-S3 / ESP32-CAM / Nano ESP32, ESP32-C3 family, CH32V003, Raspberry Pi 3B Linux.
inside.chipsBody: Define your own ICs in C, Rust, or AssemblyScript via the Wokwi Custom Chips API — drive pins, attributes, timers, I²C and SPI from your code.
inside.instrumentsBody: Multi-channel oscilloscope, voltmeter, ammeter, signal generator (sine / square / DC) — drop on any node and watch waveforms live.
inside.componentsBody: LEDs, RGB LEDs, 7-segment displays, 16×2 LCD, ILI9341 TFT, NeoPixel strips, servos, buzzers, ultrasonic sensors, DHT22, MPU6050, keypads.
inside.ideBody: Monaco code editor, multi-file workspace, Library Manager (full Arduino library index), arduino-cli compiler, Serial Monitor.

audience.heading: For teachers, students & tinkerers
audience.lead: Velxio is free under AGPLv3 — no licence cost, no per-seat pricing, no cloud account. Self-host with one Docker command for university labs.
audience.coursesBody: Use the same tool from week-1 (Ohm’s law, voltage dividers) through Arduino projects and op-amp signal chains. One UI for the whole syllabus.
audience.labsBody: Self-host on a campus server. No internet dependency, no account walls — students just open the URL.
audience.makersBody: Validate your circuit and firmware before ordering parts. The scope and DMM are already on the bench.

faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: Is Velxio a complete electronics simulator?
faq.a1: Yes. Velxio combines a SPICE-accurate analog solver, 19 simulated microcontrollers (Arduino, ESP32, RP2040, ATtiny85, Raspberry Pi 3), 100+ components, an oscilloscope, voltmeter, ammeter, and signal generator — everything you need to design and validate an electronics project end-to-end.
faq.q2: Can I use it like a virtual breadboard?
faq.a2: Yes. Drop components on the canvas, drag wires between pins, and the simulator builds the SPICE netlist automatically. Wires snap to a grid; components rotate in 90° increments; signal-type colours mark VCC, GND, digital, and analog routes.
faq.q3: Do I need to install anything?
faq.a3: No. Open velxio.dev in any modern browser and start. The SPICE solver and the AVR / RP2040 emulators run locally in your browser; Xtensa and RISC-V boards (ESP32, ESP32-C3, CH32V003) plus Raspberry Pi 3 Linux run through QEMU lcgamboa, bundled in the Docker image. Compilation of Arduino sketches uses the cloud arduino-cli backend.
faq.q4: Is it good for teaching electronics?
faq.a4: Yes — that is one of the design goals. Free, no install, no account, no licence cost. 100+ pre-wired example circuits cover voltage dividers, RC filters, op-amp amplifiers, transistor switches, rectifiers, and complete Arduino projects. Great for university labs and self-learners.
faq.q5: Is Velxio a Tinkercad / Falstad alternative?
faq.a5: Yes — and a more accurate one. Velxio runs real ngspice (the engine professional EDA tools use), and unlike those tools it also runs the firmware on the microcontroller driving the circuit. Open-source under AGPLv3.

bottom.title: Open your virtual breadboard
bottom.body: Wire your first circuit in seconds — no setup, no install, no account.

hero.title: Free Online Arduino Simulator
hero.subtitle: Write Arduino code and simulate it instantly — real AVR8 emulation at 16 MHz with 48+ interactive electronic components. No install, no cloud, no account required.
hero.trust: Free & open-source · No signup required · Runs 100% in your browser

what.heading: What can you simulate?
what.lead: Velxio simulates Arduino programs with full AVR8 CPU accuracy — including GPIO ports, hardware timers, USART, ADC, PWM, SPI, and I2C. No approximations, no shortcuts.
what.unoBody: Full ATmega328P simulation at 16 MHz. PORTB, PORTC, PORTD register emulation. All 14 digital and 6 analog pins.
what.megaBody: ATmega2560 with 256 KB flash, 54 digital pins, 16 analog inputs, and 4 hardware USART channels.
what.componentsBody: LEDs, resistors, buttons, buzzer, servo, ultrasonic sensor, ILI9341 TFT, 16×2 LCD, NeoPixel, DHT22, and more.
what.serialBodyPrefix: Real-time TX/RX with auto baud-rate detection. Send commands and view
what.multiBody: Write .ino, .h, and .cpp files in a VS Code-style Monaco editor — full multi-file Arduino project support.
what.libBody: Search and install any library from the full Arduino library index directly inside the simulator.

how.heading: How the simulator works
how.leadSuffix: — the battle-tested open-source AVR8 emulation library — to execute your compiled firmware byte-for-byte, exactly as it would run on physical hardware.
how.writeBody: Write your Arduino sketch in the Monaco editor with C++ syntax highlighting and autocomplete.
how.compileBody: Click Compile. arduino-cli produces a real .hex file — the same output as the Arduino IDE.
how.simulateBody: The .hex is loaded into the AVR8 emulator. Your program executes at 16 MHz and drives the visual components.

faq.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.a1: Yes. Velxio is completely free and open-source (GNU AGPLv3). No account, no payment, no cloud subscription — run it in your browser or self-host it with one Docker command.
faq.q2: Does the Arduino simulator work without installing anything?
faq.a2: The simulation engine runs entirely in your browser. Compiling code requires the arduino-cli backend, which you can run locally or via Docker. No IDE installation is needed.
faq.q3: What Arduino boards can I simulate?
faq.a3: Velxio supports 19 boards: Arduino Uno (ATmega328P), Arduino Nano, Arduino Mega 2560, ATtiny85, Arduino Leonardo, Arduino Pro Mini (AVR8) — plus Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040), ESP32-C3 / XIAO ESP32-C3 / CH32V003 (RISC-V), ESP32 / ESP32-S3 / ESP32-CAM (Xtensa via QEMU), and Raspberry Pi 3B (Linux via QEMU).
faq.q4: Can I simulate LEDs, sensors, and displays?
faq.a4: Yes. Velxio includes 48+ interactive wokwi-elements: LEDs, resistors, buttons, servo motors, ultrasonic sensors, ILI9341 TFT displays, LCD, NeoPixel strips, buzzers, DHT22, and more.
faq.a5: Yes. Velxio is a free, self-hosted alternative to Wokwi. It uses the same avr8js emulation library and wokwi-elements visual components, but runs entirely on your own machine with no cloud dependency.

bottom.title: Ready to simulate your Arduino?
bottom.body: Open the editor and start coding in seconds — no setup, no install, no account needed.
